story.headline: US strikes Iran for a 12th night as tanker attacks shut the Strait of Hormuz
story.summary: The US bombed Iran for a 12th consecutive night as the IRGC set a tanker ablaze and declared Hormuz closed, Houthis hit two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea, and Brent neared $100.
story.display_body: The US struck Iran for a 12th consecutive night early Thursday (23 July) as tanker attacks pushed the war into the world's oil arteries. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said an explosion set a tanker ablaze in the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes — after the vessel tried the southern route off Oman, and declared the strait completely closed to oil traffic while the strikes continue; two people were killed in Iran's Khuzestan province. In the Red Sea, Yemen's Houthis claimed cruise-missile, ballistic-missile and drone strikes on two Saudi oil tankers, the Encelia and the Layla, extending the shipping disruption to the Bab al-Mandeb strait. Brent crude neared $100 a barrel on the news, and budget airline easyJet reported an April-June pre-tax profit of £85m, down 70% from £286m a year earlier, blaming soaring fuel costs and later bookings tied to the conflict.
story.why: With both of the region's main maritime chokepoints now under fire, the war's costs are reaching ordinary consumers and airlines far from the front through fuel prices.

story.headline: US and Saudi Arabia agree landmark civilian nuclear deal, Israel-normalisation demand dropped
story.summary: Washington and Riyadh signed a civilian nuclear pact letting Saudi Arabia build reactors with US technology, dropping the prior condition of normalising ties with Israel.
story.display_body: The United States and Saudi Arabia signed a landmark civilian nuclear-cooperation pact on Thursday (23 July), dropping the long-standing demand that Riyadh normalise ties with Israel first. The deal clears the way for Saudi Arabia to build nuclear reactors using American technology; the requirement to recognise Israel — which had stalled the agreement for years — was abandoned in the final text. The Trump administration released few details, but the pact paves the way for a uranium-enrichment facility, which Israel warns could in principle be repurposed toward weapons.
story.why: Enrichment on Saudi soil is the proliferation red line the region has argued over for a decade, and signing it mid-war, without the Israel normalisation Washington had insisted on, marks a sharp shift in US Gulf policy.

story.headline: EU adopts weakened 21st Russia sanctions package after Greek LNG carve-out
story.summary: EU ambassadors agreed a 21st sanctions package on Russia after Greece won an exemption to keep exporting Russian LNG to non-EU customers.
story.display_body: EU ambassadors agreed a 21st sanctions package against Russia on Thursday morning (23 July), but only after granting Greece a carve-out to keep shipping Russian LNG. Athens had vetoed the deal until it won an exemption to continue exporting Russian liquefied natural gas (LNG) to non-EU customers, blunting a bloc-wide ban due in January 2027. The package still froze — for one year — a scheduled rise in the price cap on Russian oil that would have lifted it from $44 to $58 a barrel, which Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said prevents Moscow's war machine from profiting from the current oil-price spike; it also blacklists more shadow fleet tankers Russia uses to dodge the cap. Bulgaria stripped two names from the list — Orthodox Patriarch Kirill and Lukoil founder Vagit Alekperov — and a proposal to bar Russian soldiers from the Schengen area was watered down.
story.why: The repeated national carve-outs show how much unanimity is eroding the bite of EU sanctions even as the bloc reaches its 21st round.

story.headline: Modi pledges fast-track courts as India's exam-fraud protests grow
story.summary: Modi promised fast-track courts in his first response to the youth-led Cockroach protests over leaked medical-exam papers occupying central Delhi.
story.display_body: India's Prime Minister Narendra Modi promised fast-track courts on Thursday (23 July) in his first public response to the youth-led Cockroach protests convulsing Delhi. The movement — organised as the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) — erupted over leaked national medical-school entrance-exam papers and has occupied Parliament Street in central Delhi for three days, demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan; police have been accused of using pellet guns against demonstrators. Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared, Modi wrote on X, pledging swift punishment for those behind the paper leaks.
story.why: An exam-fraud grievance has escalated into the most sustained street challenge to Modi's government in years, testing how it handles a leaderless, student-driven movement.

story.headline: Wildfires kill three firefighters across France, Spain and Italy
story.summary: Wildfires killed three firefighters and forced thousands from their homes across France, Spain and Italy, with the largest blaze in France's Gironde.
story.display_body: Wildfires killed three firefighters and forced thousands from their homes across France, Spain and Italy this week, with the largest blaze in south-western France. In the Gironde, more than 10,000 people were evacuated overnight (Le Temps put the figure at 12,000) as a fire tore through over 2,000 hectares; some 500 firefighters with aerial support were battling it Thursday morning, and multiple southern French departments stayed under Météo-France's red fire alert. Two firefighters were killed on Tuesday (21 July) near Bordeaux and a third, named as Alessandro Marchi, died fighting fires in Sicily, where about 6,000 responders have been mobilised.
story.why: The fires are the sharp end of a Europe-wide heat-and-drought spell that is straining emergency services from the Atlantic to Sicily.

fields.display_body: Wildfires killed three firefighters and forced thousands from their homes across France, Spain and Italy this week, with the largest blaze in south-western France. story.headline: Rubio and Lavrov meet in Manila as Ukraine seeks to revive talks
story.summary: US and Russian foreign ministers held a half-hour meeting on Ukraine at the ASEAN summit in Manila, reaffirming known positions.
story.display_body: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov met for about half an hour in Manila on Thursday (23 July) on the sidelines of an ASEAN summit. Russia's foreign ministry said Lavrov reaffirmed readiness for a diplomatic settlement of the Ukraine war on the terms Presidents Trump and Putin discussed at their August 2025 Alaska meeting, while pressing that arming Kyiv was unacceptable; Rubio said afterwards the US remains ready to play a constructive role but declined to characterise Moscow's position. The meeting came as Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said he had spoken with US envoys to try to revive stalled talks.

story.headline: Strong franc weighs on Roche as H1 revenue slips 2% to CHF 30.4bn
story.summary: Roche reported first-half 2026 sales down 2% in francs but up 6% at constant rates, held back by the strong Swiss franc.
story.display_body: Roche's first-half 2026 revenue slipped 2% in francs to CHF 30.4bn as the strong franc masked underlying growth, the Basel drugmaker reported Thursday (23 July). Stripped of currency effects, sales rose 6%; the pharmaceuticals division brought in CHF 23.6bn (down 1% year on year) while diagnostics fell 3% to CHF 6.7bn, dragged by a pricing reform in China. Core operating profit dipped 1% to CHF 11.9bn (up 10% at constant exchange rates) and core earnings per share fell 2% to CHF 10.85. Roche kept its full-year targets — mid-single-digit sales growth and high-single-digit core-EPS growth, both at constant rates.
story.why: The split between the reported (-2%) and currency-adjusted (+6%) numbers is the recurring story of Swiss exporters this year — real growth abroad that a strong franc keeps shaving off the home-currency bottom line.

story.headline: Nestle spins bottled water into CHF 4.5bn joint venture with Platinum Equity
story.summary: Nestle and US fund Platinum Equity created a 50/50 water-and-premium-beverages venture, Peranel, valued at CHF 4.5bn.
story.display_body: Vaud-based Nestlé is spinning its bottled-water and premium-drinks arm — San Pellegrino, Perrier and Vaud's own Henniez — into a 50/50 joint venture with US private-equity firm Platinum Equity, the group announced Thursday (23 July). The new company, Peranel, values the division at €4.9bn (CHF 4.5bn) and hands day-to-day control to a specialist operator; the water business had been bruised by a scandal over the illegal use of filtration on mineral water sold as natural. Nestlé frames the move as a milestone in a broader refocusing on its core food and beverage brands.
story.why: It is one of the clearest steps yet in Nestlé's restructuring, and it partly hives off a Swiss household name (Henniez) into shared ownership with a US fund.

story.headline: MeteoSuisse warns Swiss drought could turn locally critical
story.summary: MeteoSuisse says the western Plateau, Jura and northern Alps face a 60-90% rainfall deficit as European drought intensifies.
story.display_body: MeteoSuisse warned Thursday (23 July) that Switzerland's drought could turn locally very critical without substantial rain, with parts of the country running a 60-90% rainfall deficit. The western Plateau, the Jura and the northern slopes of the eastern Alps are worst hit, according to the national drought platform (Plateforme nationale sur la sécheresse); rivers are dropping, Alpine pastures — including at Rossinière in Vaud's Pays-d'Enhaut — are stressed, and the shortfall feeds the same heat-and-drought that is driving the wildfires across southern Europe.
